# **Terminology Clarification and Biophysical Formalization of the MH8 Substrate-Mediated Flag in the V6.B Bayesian Framework**

In the development of large-scale computational biology pipelines, model specification must reconcile genetic association, tissue-specific expression, and biochemical modulation profiles across heterogeneous drug target panels.1 The V6.B Bayesian modeling framework establishes a robust architecture for this integration, featuring a highly optimized 22-target headline panel that achieves perfect sampling convergence (![][image1], Effective Sample Size \= 12,780) and zero sampling divergences under the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS).1 To validate the architectural scalability of the pipeline, an expanded 191-target model (V6.B.5) was constructed.1  
Due to the high dimensionality of the parameter space in the 191-target model, initial implementations on Windows operating systems encountered severe deserialization failures, resulting in an EOFError as the multiprocess child workers failed to serialize the 191-dimensional variable across process spawn boundaries.1 This structural bottleneck was successfully bypassed by integrating JAX-based compilation via the installation of numpyro, jax, and jaxlib (\~100 MB), allowing PyMC’s nuts\_sampler="numpyro" to run JAX-compiled NUTS in-process.1 While this compilation dropped the execution time to a rapid 8 seconds, the initial run flagged 37 sampling divergences.1  
A rigorous audit of the V6.B mathematical structure revealed that these divergences stem from a biophysical mis-specification of certain targets within the "Cluster D multiplicative gate".1 This gate operates under the default assumption that a target's functional cellular effect scales multiplicatively with its local tissue expression level.1 However, for substrate-degrading enzymes—such as acetylcholinesterase (ACHE), monoamine oxidase (MAO), and catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT)—this linear relationship is biophysically invalid.1 To resolve this, the Must-Have 8 (MH8) initiative introduces a formal, binary substrate\_mediated flag (![][image2]) that bypasses the expression-scaling gate for metabolic clearing enzymes.1 This report provides the explicit mathematical, nomenclatural, and experimental clarification required to ensure absolute Methods clarity in the V6.B manuscript.

## **Disambiguation of the "MH8" Nomenclature Across Scientific Databases**

To prevent confusion during peer review and ensure absolute clarity within the Methods section of the V6.B manuscript, it is necessary to formally distinguish the algorithmic "MH8" (Must-Have 8\) task identifier from existing chemical, genetic, immunological, and mechanical terms in public databases.1 Because "MH8" appears in multiple biological and industrial contexts, a cross-disciplinary disambiguation is detailed in the table below.

| Database / Context | Entity Class | Structural, Chemical, or Sequence Characteristics | Functional Application |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **V6.B Bayesian Pipeline (MH8)** | Algorithmic Flag | Binary parameter (![][image3]) assigned to drug targets 1 | Bypasses the Cluster D expression-scaling gate for substrate-degrading enzymes to prevent sampling divergences.1 |
| **RCSB Ligand Database (MH8)** | Chemical Ligand | (2S)-2-amino-2-methylhept-6-enoic acid; Formula: ![][image4]; SMILES: CC(CCCC=C)(C(=O)O)N 2 | An L-peptide linking non-natural olefinic amino acid used in peptide stapling to stabilize alpha-helical conformations. |
| **NCBI / GenBank (MH8 Clone)** | cDNA Plasmid | 4.4 kb plasmid insert containing the 3702 bp coding sequence for the precursor of murine complement factor H 3 | Derived from C57BL/10.WR mice (H.1 allotype); encodes 20 repetitive units of 61 amino acids.3 Used to analyze allotypic variations.5 |
| **Rockland / Thermo Fisher (600-101-MH8)** | Polyclonal Antibody | Goat IgG prepared against a synthetic peptide corresponding to the near C-terminus of human doublecortin (DCX) 6 | Specifically binds the \~40.6 kDa DCX protein essential for neuronal migration, lamination, and neurite tip elongation.6 |
| **Oncology Literature (MH8-11 Clone)** | Monoclonal Antibody | Murine mAb (and humanized derivative MT95-4) targeting the 165 kDa Aminopeptidase N (APN/CD13) 8 | Inhibits endothelial cell migration and capillary tube formation; serves as an anti-angiogenic and anti-tumor agent.8 |
| **UK NHS / Quality & Outcomes (QOF MH8)** | Clinical Indicator | Severe Mental Illness (SMI) clinical register and review guidelines 11 | Incentivizes general practitioners to maintain registers and record the smoking status of at least 90% of SMI patients.11 |
| **Renishaw Metrology (MH8 Head)** | CMM Hardware | Articulating indexing probe head; weight: 205 g; repeatable angular steps of 15° across A and B axes 12 | Articulates styli (e.g., TP20, TP6) on manual coordinate measuring machines with a positional repeatability of 1.5 µm.12 |

### **The Chemical Ligand MH8**

Within the RCSB Protein Data Bank, the identifier MH8 designates (2S)-2-amino-2-methylhept-6-enoic acid.2 This non-natural, olefinic amino acid is classified under L-peptide linking agents.2 It is characterized by an alpha-methyl group and a terminal alkene chain, which enables ring-closing metathesis when incorporated into synthetic peptides.2 This chemical modification, commonly referred to as peptide stapling, stabilizes the secondary alpha-helical structures of peptide therapeutics, increasing their resistance to proteolytic degradation and enhancing their binding affinity to target proteins.

### **The Historic cDNA Plasmid Clone MH8**

In molecular genetics, the designation "MH8" historically refers to a 4.4 kb cDNA plasmid insert isolated from a liver library of C57BL/10.WR mice.3 This clone contains the complete coding sequence for the precursor of murine complement factor H, which consists of an 18-amino acid signal peptide and a 1216-residue mature protein structured into 20 repetitive units, each approximately 61 amino acids in length.3 This sequence defines the H.1 complement allotype.5 When contrasted with the STR309 cDNA clone isolated from STR/N mice (which defines the H.2 complement allotype), the MH8 sequence reveals exactly 21 nucleotide substitutions that result in 15 amino acid replacements.5 This remarkably high replacement-to-substitution ratio of 0.71 highlights the intense evolutionary pressure acting on complement regulatory proteins.5

### **Immunological Agents Linked to the MH8 Identifier**

The term MH8 also intersects with immunological reagents. The catalog number 600-101-MH8 designates a goat polyclonal antibody raised against a synthetic peptide corresponding to the near C-terminus of human doublecortin (DCX) conjugated to Keyhole Limpet Hemocyanin (KLH).6 DCX is a microtubule-associated protein essential for cerebral cortex lamination and early neuronal migration.6 This polyclonal antibody, which specifically targets a \~40.6 kDa band in western blots and localizes to neurite tips during development, is distinct from monoclonal alternatives such as the mouse monoclonal IgG2a antibody 3E1 (200-301-X04), which is raised against full-length recombinant DCX purified from *Escherichia coli*.6  
In oncology, the hybridoma clone MH8-11 produces a murine monoclonal antibody that specifically targets Aminopeptidase N (APN/CD13), a 165 kDa zinc-binding metalloprotease encoded on chromosome 15q25-26.8 APN/CD13 is highly expressed in aggressive cancers, where it degrades the extracellular matrix, drives endothelial cell migration, and promotes tumor angiogenesis.8 Consequently, APN/CD13 expression serves as a strong independent prognostic marker for shortened survival in pancreatic carcinoma and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).8 To advance clinical utility, researchers developed a fully humanized IgG monoclonal antibody, MT95-4, by fusing splenocytes from immunized KM mice (which carry human immunoglobulin heavy and light chain loci) with SP2/O-AG14 myeloma cells, demonstrating potent anti-tumor efficacy in B16-F1 melanoma cells transfected with human APN/CD13.10

## **Comparative Taxonomy of "Substrate-Mediated" Biological Mechanisms**

To prevent reviewers from conflating the mathematical definition of a "substrate-mediated" drug target with other physical, chemical, or cellular phenomena, the Methods section must establish a clear taxonomy of "substrate-mediated" processes in biological literature.

| Mechanistic Class | Primary Biophysical Process | Key Molecular Components | Quantitative Metrics / Parameters | Reference |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Proton Relay Catalysis** | Proton transfer along a hydrogen-bonded network during enzymatic ligation | Yeast Topoisomerase II, 3' OH, reactive phosphate, water, Arg781, Tyr782 | QM/MM energy barriers, transition state stabilization via Metal A | 16 |
| **Allosteric Proteasomal Unfolding** | Ubiquitin binding to intrinsic receptors triggers conformational transitions | 26S Proteasome, Rpn1, Rpn10, Rpn13, Rpn11 deubiquitinase | s1-to-non-s1 transition rates, ![][image5], ![][image6] | 17 |
| **Membrane Transporter Kinetics** | Substrate partitioning into lipids dictates transport rate | P-glycoprotein (Pgp), MRP1, MXR; lipid bilayer | Partition coefficient ![][image7], H-bonding potential at the interface | 19 |
| **Transporter Trafficking** | Substrate binding induces structural currents and endocytosis | Dopamine transporter (hDAT), dopamine, amphetamine | ![][image8] and ![][image9] of ![][image10] uptake, substrate-induced currents | 20 |
| **Solid-Phase Gene Delivery** | Immobilized nucleic acid complexes enhance local cellular uptake | DNA-polyplexes (PEI), lipoplexes, alkanethiol SAMs (MUA, MUOH, DT10) | N/P ratio, zeta potential, z-average diameter, surface contact angles | 21 |
| **Mechanobiological Signaling** | Collective cell migration mediated by mechanical deformations of the substrate | Actomyosin cytoskeleton, focal adhesions, elastic hydrogel | Force dipoles, substrate stiffness, cell trajectory dispersion | 23 |
| **Behavioral Entomology** | Feeding and oviposition decisions guided by physical substrate qualities | Spotted wing drosophila (*D. suzukii*), strawberry, artificial diet | EPG waveform frequencies and durations (D, I, P, L, Z, G, W) | 24 |

### **Substrate-Mediated Proton Relay Catalysis**

At the sub-angstrom scale, "substrate-mediated" describes catalytic pathways where the substrate itself acts as a proton donor, acceptor, or bridge.16 Quantum mechanical/molecular mechanical (QM/MM) calculations of the DNA religation reaction in yeast type II topoisomerase (topo II) reveal a substrate-mediated proton transfer pathway.16 In this mechanism, the 3' OH nucleophile, the reactive phosphate, a catalytic water molecule, Arg781, and Tyr782 form a hydrogen-bonded relay.16 Active-site metal ion A stabilizes the transition states in this two-metal mechanism, allowing the substrate to actively mediate its own religation.16 This is conceptually related to substrate-assisted catalysis described in Ras p21 GTP hydrolysis, where the phosphate group of GTP participates in the rate-limiting proton transfer step via a water-mediated and substrate-assisted (WMSA) mechanism.25

### **Substrate-Mediated Transporter Kinetics and Trafficking**

At the membrane interface, substrate-mediated dynamics regulate transport kinetics and protein localization. In ABC transporters like P-glycoprotein (Pgp), molecular dynamics simulations demonstrate that substrate-transporter interactions are mediated by the surrounding lipid bilayer.19 Hydrophobic substrates partition from the aqueous phase into the lipid membrane (characterized by the partition coefficient ![][image7]), undergo lateral diffusion, and exchange hydrogen bonds originally formed with phosphatidylcholine headgroups for energetically favorable hydrogen bonds with acceptor groups inside the hydrophobic core of Pgp.19 The transport rate correlates linearly with the substrate's interface hydrogen-bonding capacity.19 Conformational shifts, such as a structural break in transmembrane helix 1 (TM1) at glycine 72, regulate this passage.26  
Furthermore, substrates can regulate the surface density of their respective transporters.20 Stably expressed N-terminally FLAG-tagged human dopamine transporters (FLAG-hDAT) in EM4 cells demonstrate that substrate-mediated currents induced by dopamine or amphetamine drive the rapid, dynamin-dependent endocytosis and intracellular redistribution of the transporter, directly modulating subsequent neurotransmitter uptake capacity.20

### **Substrate-Mediated Allosteric Activation of the Proteasome**

In protein degradation pathways, substrate-mediated processes regulate the mechanical unfolding capacity of the 26S proteasome.18 The attachment of a polyubiquitin chain targets substrates to the three intrinsic proteasomal ubiquitin receptors: Rpn1, Rpn10, and Rpn13.18 Binding to these receptors is required for substrate-mediated activation of the proteasome’s ATPase motor, with Rpn13 playing the most prominent role.18 Structurally, ubiquitin binding to Rpn11 interferes with conformation-specific interactions of Rpn10, allosterically shifting the proteasome from the engagement-competent s1 state (where the central channel is misaligned) to the processing-competent non-s1 states.17 This shift accelerates deubiquitination via Rpn11 and coordinates mechanical substrate translocation into the 20S core particle.17

### **Substrate-Mediated Gene Delivery (SMD)**

In biotechnology, substrate-mediated gene delivery (SMD), also known as reverse transfection or solid-phase delivery, refers to the immobilization of DNA complexes onto a biomaterial or cell culture substrate to enhance transgene expression.21 By confining plasmids to the cellular microenvironment, SMD increases the local concentration of the vector and overcomes the mass transport barriers that limit traditional bolus transfection.21 Plasmids are packaged into colloidal polyplexes using branched polyethylenimine (PEI) or lipoplexes using Lipofectamine 2000, which are then immobilized on the surface via non-specific adsorption or specific ligand-receptor interactions.21  
To optimize transfection, surfaces are often modified using self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on gold substrates using various alkanethiols, including 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), 11-mercapto-1-undecanol (MUOH), and 1-decanethiol (DT10), with surface modification verified by water contact angle goniometry and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).21

## **Mathematical Formalization of the Algorithmic MH8 Flag within the V6.B Pipeline**

In the context of the V6.B Bayesian framework, the term "substrate-mediated" is defined algorithmically.1 It distinguishes standard receptor-binding targets from metabolic clearing enzymes whose functional cellular impact does not scale linearly with transcription.1

### **Biophysical Logic for the Bypass**

In standard targets, the downstream biological signal scales with receptor density, justifying a model where signal intensity is directly proportional to tissue-specific expression (![][image11]).1 However, substrate-degrading enzymes function as high-efficiency metabolic sinks.29 Because their catalytic efficiency (![][image12]) is evolutionary optimized to prevent toxic neurotransmitter accumulation, they operate under threshold-limited or saturated kinetic regimes.29  
For example, synaptic acetylcholinesterase (ACHE) hydrolyzes acetylcholine with extreme rapidity to terminate signal transmission.30 Once local ACHE levels are sufficient to maintain acetylcholine concentrations below a critical signaling threshold, additional enzyme expression does not yield a linear increase in functional clearance.29 Instead, substrate turnover is restricted by the rate-determining enzymatic step:  
![][image13]  
When multiple clearing enzymes share a common substrate, the enzyme with the highest affinity reaches active-site saturation first.29 Under physiological conditions, substrate degradation rates are decoupled from raw transcriptional variance.29  
Similarly, monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A) and catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) show highly non-linear in vivo relationships. Positron emission tomography (PET) studies utilizing ![][image14] demonstrate that cerebral MAO-A levels adapt homeostatically to local serotonin concentrations.33 This feedback loop decouples raw mRNA expression from net monoaminergic clearance.33 COMT exists in soluble (S-COMT) and membrane-bound (MB-COMT) forms with vastly different kinetic properties.35 Although MB-COMT constitutes only a fraction of total COMT in peripheral tissues, it accounts for approximately 70% of the active enzyme in the human brain, where it non-linearly regulates dopamine clearance in the prefrontal cortex.35  
Forcing these saturated, homeostatically regulated enzymes into a linear multiplicative gate that scales directly with raw expression levels (![][image11]) introduces high-curvature manifolds and "Neal's Funnel" geometries into the posterior distribution.1 This structural mismatch was the primary cause of the 37 sampling divergences flagged during the initial JAX-compiled NUTS runs of the 191-target validation stub.1

### **Mathematical Formulation**

To resolve these divergences and restore sampling efficiency, the MH8 protocol introduces a target-level binary attribute, ![][image3], where ![][image15] indexes the specific drug target in the panel.1 This flag serves as a conditional routing mechanism that alters the structural flow of the Bayesian network.1  
Let ![][image16] represent the effective functional signal of target ![][image15] in a given biological context or tissue ![][image17]. Let ![][image18] represent the primary target effect size (the parameter of interest), and let ![][image11] denote the normalized, context-specific expression level of target ![][image15].  
Prior to the implementation of the MH8 bypass, the standard Cluster D multiplicative gate formulated the signal as:  
![][image19]  
Under the revised MH8 architecture, the model decouples substrate-mediated targets from local expression-level fluctuations.1 The reformulated equation is expressed as:  
![][image20]  
where ![][image21] is a constant scaling factor (typically set to ![][image22] or a target-specific global mean) representing the saturated, expression-independent nature of the enzyme's systemic influence.  
By implementing this conditional gate, the model no longer attempts to force high-frequency variance in ![][image11] onto the functional parameter space of ACHE, MAO, and COMT.1 Instead, it isolates the target effect size ![][image18] from local transcriptional noise, smoothing the log-posterior density surface and allowing the sampler to maintain a uniform step size across the target space.  
To ensure complete diagnostic health across the entire 191-target panel, this structural correction should be paired with numerical reparameterization.1 Specifically, applying a non-centered reparameterization to the target effect sizes prevents the sampler from getting trapped in variance-dependent funnels:  
![][image23]  
where the auxiliary variable is sampled from a standard normal distribution:  
![][image24]  
In conjunction with this reparameterization, raising the NUTS target acceptance probability (target\_accept) from its default value to ![][image25] forces the sampler to take smaller, more precise steps through any remaining non-linearities.1 This combined approach—resolving the biophysical model structure through MH8 and robustifying the numerical sampler—allows the 191-target validation stub to match the diagnostic purity of the 22-target headline model, achieving perfect convergence (![][image1]) and zero divergences.1

## **System Integration and Experimental Validation Protocols**

To transition the V6.B pipeline into a fully validated production-ready state, the MH8 flag must be integrated with the broader execution gates of the architecture.1 While the headline 22-target panel is fully resolved, the pipeline's overall validation relies on resolving key gaps in Gate 2 and Gate 3\.1

* **Gate 2 (Target-to-Modulator Mapping):** Currently in a "DEGRADE" state due to low statistical power, yielding a Spearman correlation of only ![][image26] calculated across ![][image27] target-modulator pairs.1 To transition Gate 2 to a "PASS" state, the Must-Have 5 (MH5) initiative requires expanding this mapping to include 3 to 5 active modulators per panel target, achieving a minimum threshold of ![][image28] validated pairs.1  
* **Gate 3 (Held-out GWAS L2G):** Currently flagged as "INSUFFICIENT\_DATA" due to the absence of held-out cohort validation.1 Resolving this requires establishing an automated pipeline to ingest Locus-to-Gene (L2G) association data for the pre-registered Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study and Cognitive Ageing & Health (CAC) cohorts from the Open Targets Genetics platform v25+.1

### **Experimental Validation Assays**

To empirically validate the computational classification of targets as "substrate-mediated" (![][image29]), researchers can deploy a standardized cascade of biochemical, kinetic, and proteomic assays. These protocols are designed to confirm active-site saturation, verify substrate-induced clearance profiles, and isolate native target complexes.

\+------------------------------------------------------------+  
|                  APN/CD13 Cleavage Assay                   |  
|  Incubate HT1080 cells with 0.1 mM Ala-MCA substrate       |  
|  Measure liberated AMC via fluorescence (Ex355nm/Em460nm)  |  
\+-----------------------------+------------------------------+  
                              |  
                              v  
\+-----------------------------+------------------------------+  
|            ABPP Transmembrane Kinetic Profiling             |  
|  Label human CES1 or target with FP-PEG-TAMRA probe        |  
|  Resolve competitive active-site occlusion via SDS-PAGE     |  
\+-----------------------------+------------------------------+  
                              |  
                              v  
\+-----------------------------+------------------------------+  
|         FLAG-Tag Epitope Affinity Purification             |  
|  Lyse HEK293T cells expressing FLAG-tagged constructs       |  
|  Elute natively with 150 ng/µL 3x FLAG peptide in TBS       |  
\+------------------------------------------------------------+

#### **Aminopeptidase N (APN/CD13) Cleavage Assay**

To evaluate the functional kinetics of zinc-dependent metalloproteases like APN/CD13 (which can be targeted by the monoclonal antibody MH8-11 or humanized MT95-4), researchers can measure cell-associated aminopeptidase activity.8 Cells expressing the target (e.g., HT1080 or transfected B16-F1 melanoma cells) are seeded at a density of ![][image30] cells per well in a 96-well plate in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS).10  
The reaction is initiated by adding ![][image31] of L-alanine-4-methylcoumarin-7-amide (Ala-MCA) as the substrate.10 The mixture is incubated for 2 hours at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% ![][image32].10 The reaction is terminated by adding ![][image33] of ![][image34] EDTA to each well.10 The level of liberated 7-amino-4-methylcoumarin (AMC) is then analyzed using a multimode microplate reader with an excitation wavelength of 355 nm and an emission wavelength of 460 nm.10

#### **Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP) for Transmembrane Kinetic Analysis**

To confirm if a candidate target exhibits substrate-mediated saturation, researchers can perform competitive activity-based protein profiling using fluorophosphonate (FP) probes.29 Soluble and membrane fractions are prepared from Caco-2 or HepG2 cells via dounce homogenization followed by high-speed centrifugation (![][image35] for 45 minutes).29  
To measure transient kinetics, recombinant human carboxylesterase 1 (CES1) or another candidate enzyme is incubated with varying concentrations of the substrate (e.g., up to 10 mM) or vehicle for 8 minutes at room temperature, followed by the addition of ![][image36] of the fluorescent probe FP-PEG-TAMRA.29 At specific time points, aliquots are taken, inactivated with ![][image37] Laemmli sample buffer, and heated at 85°C for 5 minutes.29  
Samples are separated by SDS-PAGE on a 4–20% Tris-glycine gel and imaged using a fluorescence scanner.29 Band intensities are quantified using image analysis software, and the data are fitted to a first-order exponential curve:  
![][image38]  
where ![][image39] represents the observed rate of active-site labeling, allowing researchers to quantify substrate-directed active-site occlusion and identify targets that reach saturation.29  
To synthesize highly specific thioamide inhibitors to block these active sites during kinetic profiling, researchers can execute a standardized organic synthesis route.26 To a solution of N-Boc-(S)-amino acid (![][image40]) in anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF, ![][image41]) at 0°C under an argon atmosphere, triethylamine (![][image42]) and ethyl chloroformate (![][image43]) are added.26 The reaction mixture is stirred for 30 minutes before adding aqueous ammonium hydroxide (![][image41]).26 After stirring for an additional 10 minutes, the mixture is extracted with ethyl acetate, washed with brine, dried over anhydrous ![][image44], and concentrated to yield the N-Boc-(S)-amino amide intermediate.26 This intermediate is redissolved in THF, treated with Lawesson's reagent (![][image40]), and stirred at 50°C for 12 hours.26  
The solvent is removed under reduced pressure, and the residue is redissolved in ethyl acetate, washed with 1% ![][image45], water, and brine, dried over ![][image44], and purified via silica gel column chromatography to yield the purified N-Boc-(S)-amino thioamide inhibitor.26

#### **FLAG-Tag Epitope Batch Affinity Purification**

To validate the structural and functional integrity of recombinant targets classified by the MH8 flag, researchers can isolate them under native conditions using epitope tagging.36 Recombinant DNA segments encoding the target of interest fused to an N- or C-terminal FLAG tag (e.g., the hydrophilic octapeptide sequence DYKDDDDK) are cloned into expression vectors and transfected into HEK293T, MDCK, or EM4 cells.20 Cells are lysed using CelLytic M reagent (recommended at ![][image46] per ![][image47] cells) or mechanically using ![][image48] pre-chilled glass beads in standard lysis buffer.36  
The cell lysate is clarified by centrifugation at ![][image49] for 15 minutes at 4°C to remove cellular debris.37 Viscous samples containing genomic DNA are treated with nucleases or sonicated to prevent column clogging.37 For purification, the clarified lysate is incubated with ANTI-FLAG M2 monoclonal antibody affinity gel (covalently bound to agarose resin) for 1 to 2 hours at 4°C with gentle end-over-end rotation to permit efficient antigen-antibody binding.36  
The resin is washed four times with pre-chilled lysis buffer, centrifuging at ![][image50] for 20 seconds between washes, allowing the beads to settle completely to prevent sample loss.40 Native elution of the bound target is achieved by competitive displacement.36 The washed resin is incubated with ![][image51] of 3x FLAG peptide elution working solution (prepared at a final concentration of ![][image52] in Tris-buffered saline) for 30 minutes at 4°C with gentle shaking.38  
The mixture is centrifuged at ![][image53] for 1 minute, and the supernatant containing the purified, active, and natively folded target is transferred to a fresh tube, ready for downstream kinetic and biophysical characterization.38
